title: 20-Mile March

content.statement: Hit consistent performance targets in good times and bad.
content.question: What is your non-negotiable monthly minimum, and have you hit it every month this year?
content.equation: Long-term Output = Daily Floor × Days Hit per Year × Years
content.mental_model: Daily march through any weather, pace beats heroics.
content.business_lens: Set a quantifiable monthly minimum (revenue, output, learning) and never miss it; cap the upside to avoid burnout.
content.life_lens: Build a daily minimum you hit on great days and bad days alike.
sop: Define a quantifiable target per period
Commit to never miss the floor
Cap heroic over-performance to protect sustainability
content.explainer: Amundsen reached the South Pole by marching 15–20 miles daily regardless of weather; Scott raced when sunny and rested when bad and lost the race and his life. Disciplined pace beats sporadic intensity in long-horizon games.
questions: What is your non-negotiable monthly minimum, and have you hit it every month this year?
What would you have to say no to on your best month to protect the pace on your worst?
Which of the last 12 months did you miss your floor, and what caused it?

definitions.def: A discipline of hitting a consistent, predefined performance floor in both good conditions and bad, rather than sprinting when times are easy and stalling when they're hard.

content.equation_example: Daily Floor $500, Consistency 95% (≈347 of 365 days hit), sustained over 5 years. Long-term Output = $500 × 347 × 5 = $867,500. Rising consistency and more years compounding means durable, predictable growth; falling consistency means you're relying on heroics instead of a system.
content.closing_statement: Pace beats heroics over a long horizon.

caption:
Principle Nº 277: 20-Mile March

Hit consistent performance targets in good times and bad.

Ask yourself: What is your non-negotiable monthly minimum, and have you hit it every month this year?
